cards.text: The six comparison operators are: {{c1::==}}, {{c2::!=}}, {{c3::<}}, {{c4::>}}, {{c5::<=}}, and {{c6::>=}}.

cards.back: Comparison operators should return bool. true if the comparison is true, false otherwise. They should not modify either operand and should take parameters by const reference.

cards.question: What does this comparison operator do?
cards.answer: Compares two Cents objects for equality by comparing their m_cents values. Returns true if equal, false otherwise.
cards.explanation: Implemented as friend function for symmetry. Takes const references, returns bool.

cards.text: Comparison operators should be implemented as {{c1::friend}} or {{c2::normal}} functions to allow implicit conversions on both operands.

cards.front: Can you implement operator!= in terms of operator==?
cards.back: Yes. Common pattern: bool operator!=(const T& lhs, const T& rhs) { return !(lhs == rhs); }. This ensures consistency and reduces code duplication.

cards.text: In C++20, if you define operator{{c1::<=>}} (three-way comparison), the compiler can automatically generate {{c2::all six}} comparison operators.

cards.back: Two const reference parameters: bool operator<(const T& lhs, const T& rhs). Both const because comparison doesn't modify operands. Reference for efficiency.

cards.question: How would you implement operator> using operator<?
cards.answer: bool operator>(const Cents& c1, const Cents& c2) { return c2 < c1; }
cards.explanation: a > b is equivalent to b < a. Implementing in terms of operator< ensures consistency.

cards.concept: Minimizing code duplication in comparison operators
cards.explanation:
You can implement all six comparison operators from just one or two:
  • Implement operator== and operator<
  • operator!= returns !(a == b)
  • operator> returns b < a
  • operator<= returns !(b < a)
  • operator>= returns !(a < b)
Or in C++20, just implement operator<=> and operator==.
examples: bool operator!=(const T& a, const T& b) { return !(a == b); }
bool operator>(const T& a, const T& b) { return b < a; }

cards.text: All comparison operators should take parameters by {{c1::const reference}} and return {{c2::bool}}.

cards.front: Why implement comparison operators as friend functions instead of member functions?
cards.back: Friend functions allow implicit conversions on both operands and provide symmetry. With member functions, a < 5 works but 5 < a doesn't. Friend functions treat both sides equally.

cards.front: What is the three-way comparison operator in C++20?
cards.back: operator<=> (spaceship operator). It returns std::strong_ordering, std::weak_ordering, or std::partial_ordering. The compiler can use it to generate all six comparison operators automatically.
